 God, folowed by the whole list of their names.
 When they return from battle they shal write on their standards,
 Honour of God, Majesty of God, Splendour of God, Glory of God,
 together with the whole list of their names.
 The Rule for the standards of the congregation
 When they set out for battle they shal write on the first standard
 Congregation of God, on the second standard Camps of God, on the
 third standard Tribes of God, on the fourth standard Clans of God, on
 the fifth standard Divisions of God, on the sixth standard Assembly of
 God, on the seventh standard The Caled of God, on the eighth
 standard Hosts of God; and they shal write the list of their names with
 al their order.
 When they approach for battle they shal write on their standards,
 War of God, Vengeance of God, Trial of God, Reward of God, Power
 of God, Retributions of God, Might of God, Extermination of God for
 al the Nations of Vanity; and they shal write on them the whole list of
 their names.
 When they return from battle they shal write on their standards,
 Salvation of God, Victory of God, Help of God, Support of God, Joy of
God, Thanksgivings of God, Praise of God, Peace of God.
 [The measurements of the standards.] The standard of the whole
 congregation shal be fourteen cubits long; the standard [of the three
 tribes,] thirteen cubits long; [the standard of the tribe,] twelve cubits;
 [the standard of the Myriad], eleven cubits; [the standard of the
 Thousand, ten cubits; the standard of the Hundred,] nine cubits; [the
 standard of the Fifty, eight] cubits; the standard of the Ten, s[even
 cubits] ...
 V And on the sh[ield of] the Prince of al the congregation they shal
 write his name, together with the names of Israel, Levi and Aaron, and
 the names of the twelve tribes of Israel according to the order of their
 precedence, with the names of their twelve chiefs.
 The Rule for the ordering of the batle divisions to complete a front
 formation when their host has reached its fu l number
 The formation shal consist of one thousand men ranked seven lines
 deep, each man standing behind the other.
 They shal al hold shields of bronze burnished like mirrors. The
 shield shal be edged with an interlaced border and with inlaid
 ornament, a work of art in pure gold and silver and bronze and
 precious stones, a many-coloured design worked by a craftsman. The
 length of the shield shal be two and a half cubits and its width one and
 a half cubits.
 In their hands they shal hold a spear and a sword. The length of the
 spear shal be seven cubits, of which the socket and spike shal
 measure half a cubit. The socket shal be edged with three embossed
 interlaced rings of pure gold and silver and bronze, a work of art. The
 inlaid ornaments on both edges of the ring shal be bordered with
 precious stones - patterned bands worked by a craftsman - and
 (embossed) with ears of corn. Between the rings, the socket shal be
 embossed with artistry like a pi lar. The spike shal be made of bri liant
 white iron, the work of a craftsman; in its centre, pointing towards the
 tip, sha l be ears of corn in pure gold.
 The swords shal be made of pure iron refined by the smelter and
blanched to resemble a mirror, the work of a craftsman; on both sides
 (of their blades) pointing towards the tip, figured ears of corn shal be
 embossed in pure gold, and they shal have two straight borders on
 each side. The length of the sword shal be one and a half cubits and
 its width four fingers. The width of the scabbard shal be four thumbs.
 There shal be four palms to the scabbard (from the girdle), and it shal
 be attached (to the girdle) on both sides for a length of five palms (?).
 The hilt of the sword shal be of pure horn worked by a craftsman, with
 patterned bands in gold and silver and precious stones... vacat
 When... shal stand, the ... they shal order the seven battle divisions,
 division after division... thirty cubits where the me[n of the division] sha l
 stand...
 VI seven times and shal return to their positions.
 And after them, three divisions of foot-soldiers shal advance and
 shal station themselves between the formations, and the first division
 shal hurl seven javelins of war towards the enemy formation. On the
 point of the javelins they shal write, ShiningJavelinof the Power of
 God; and on the darts of the second division they shal write, Bloody
 Spikes to Bring Down the Slain by the Wrath of God; and on the
 javelins of the third division they sha l write, Flaming Blade to Devour
 the Wicked Struck Down by the Judgement of God. Al these shal hurl
 their javelins seven times and shal afterwards return to their positions.
 Then two divisions of foot-soldiers shal advance and shal station
 themselves between the two formations. The first division shal be
 armed with a spear and a shield, and the second with a shield and a
 sword, to bring down the slain by the judgement of God, and to bend
 the enemy formation by the power of God, to pay the reward of their
 wickedness to al the nations of vanity. And sovereignty shal be to the
 God of Israel, and He shal accomplish mighty deeds by the saints of
 his people.
 Seven troops of horsemen shal also station themselves to the right
 and to the left of the formation; their troops shal stand on this (side)
 and on that, seven hundred horsemen on one flank and seven hundred
horsemen on the other. Two hundred horsemen shal advance with the
 thousand men of the formation of foot-soldiers; and they shal likewise
 station themselves on both [flanks] of the camp. Altogether there shal
 be four thousand six hundred (men), and one thousand cavalrymen
 with the men of the army formations, fifty to each formation. The
 horsemen, together with the cavalry of the army shal number six
 thousand: five hundred to each tribe.
 The horses advancing into battle with the foot-soldiers shal al be
 sta lions; they sha l be swift, sensitive of mouth, and sound of wind, and
 of the required age, trained for war, and accustomed to noise and to
 every (kind of) sight. Their riders shal be galant fighting men and
 ski led horsemen, and their age shal be from thirty to forty-five years.
 The horsemen of the army shal be from forty to fifty years old. They
 [and their mounts shal wear breast-plates,] helmets, and greaves; they
 shal carry in their hands bucklers, and a spear [eight cubits] long. [The
 horseman advancing with the foot-soldiers shal carry] bows and
 arrows and javelins of war. They shal al hold themselves prepared ...
 of God and to spil the blood of the wicked ...
 VI The men of the army shal be from forty to fifty years old. The
 inspectors of the camps shal be from fifty to sixty years old. The
 officers sha l be from forty to fifty years old. The despoilers of the slain,
 the plunderers of booty, the cleansers of the land, the keepers of the
 baggage, and those who furnish the provisions shal be from twenty
five to thirty years old.
 No boy or woman shal enter their camps, from the time they leave
 Jerusalem and march out to war until they return. No man who is lame,
 or blind, or crippled, or afflicted with a lasting bodily blemish, or smitten
 with a bodily impurity, none of these shal march out to war with them.
 They shal al be freely enlisted for war, perfect in spirit and body and
 prepared for the Day of Vengeance. And no man shal go down with
 them on the day of battle who is impure because of his ‘fount’, for the
 holy angels shal be with their hosts. And there shal be a space of
 about two thousand cubits between al their camps for the place
serving as a latrine, so that no indecent nakedness may be seen in the
 surroundings of their camps.
 When the battle formations are marshaled facing the enemy
 formation facing formation, seven Priests of the sons of Aaron shal
 advance from the middle gates to the place between the formations.
 They shal be clothed in vestments of white cloth of flax, in a fine linen
 tunic and fine linen breeches; and they shal be girdled with fine cloth of
 flax embroidered with blue, purple, and scarlet thread, a many
coloured design worked by a craftsman. And on their heads they shal
 wear mitred turbans. These shal be battle raiment; they shal not take
 them into the Sanctuary.
 The first Priest shal advance before the men of the formation to
 strengthen their hand for battle, and the six other Priests shal hold in
 their hands the trumpets of Summons, and the trumpets of the
 Reminder, and the trumpets of Alarm (for massacre), and the trumpets
 of Pursuit, and the trumpets of Retreat. And when the Priests advance
 to the place between the formations, seven Levites shal accompany
 them bearing in their hands seven rams’ horns; and three officers of
 the Levites shal walk before the Priests and Levites. The Priests shal
 sound the two trumpets of Sum[mons for the gates of] war to open fifty
 shields (wide) and the foot-soldiers shal advance, fifty from one gate
 [and fifty from the other. With them shal advance] the officers of the
 Levites, and they shal advance with every formation according to al
 this R[ule].
 [The Priests shal sound the trumpets, and two divisions of foot-]
 soldiers [sha l advance] from the gate [and shal] station [themselves]
 between the two [formations] ... V I the trumpets shal sound to direct
 the slingers until they have cast seven times. Afterwards, the Priests
 shal sound for them the trumpets of Retreat and they shal return to the
 flank of the first formation to take up their position.
 Then the Priests shal sound the trumpets of Summons and three
 divisions of foot-soldiers sha l advance from the gates and shal station
 themselves between the formations; the horsemen shal be on their
 flanks, to the right and to the left. The Priests shal sound a sustained
 blast on the trumpets for battle array, and the columns shal move to
their (battle) array, each man to his place. And when they have taken
 up their stand in three arrays, the Priests shal sound a second signal,
 soft and sustained, for them to advance until they are close to the
 enemy formation. They shal seize their weapons, and the Priests shal
 then blow a shril staccato blast on the six trumpets of Massacre to
 direct the battle, and the Levites and al the blowers of rams’ horns
 shal sound a mighty alarm to terrify the heart of the enemy, and
 therewith the javelins shal fly out to bring down the slain. Then the
 sound of the horns shal cease, but the Priests shal continue to blow a
 shri l staccato blast on the trumpets to direct the battle until they have
 thrown seven times against the enemy formation. And then they shal
 sound a soft, a sustained, and a shril sound on the trumpets of
 Retreat.
 It is according to this Rule that the Priests shal sound the trumpets
 for the three divisions. With the first throw, the [Priests] sha l sound [on
 the trumpets] a mighty alarm to direct the ba[ttle until they have thrown
 seven times. Then] the Priests [shal sound] for them on the trumpets
 [of Retreat a soft, a sustained, and a shri l sound, and they shal return]
 to their positions in the formation.
 [Then the Priests shal blow the trumpets of Summons and the two
 divisions of foot-soldiers sha l advance from the gates] and shal stand
 [between the formations. And the Priests shal then blow the trumpets
 of] Massacre, [and the Levites and al the blowers of rams’ horns shal
 sound an alarm, a mighty blast, and therewith] IX they shal set about to
 bring down the slain with their hands. Al the people shal cease their
 clamour but the Priests shal continue to blow the trumpets of
 Massacre to direct the battle until the enemy is smitten and put to flight;
 and the Priests shal blow to direct the battle.
 And when they are smitten before them, the Priests shal sound the
 trumpets of Summons and al the foot-soldiers shal raly to them from
 the midst of the front formations, and the six divisions, together with the
 fighting division, sha l take up their stations. Altogether, they sha l be
 seven formations: twenty-eight thousand fighting men and six thousand
 horsemen.
 Al these shal pursue the enemy to destroy him in an everlasting
 destruction in the battle of God. The Priests shal sound for them the
trumpets of Pursuit, and they shal deploy against al the enemy in a
 pursuit to destruction; and the horsemen shal thrust them back on the
 flanks of the battle until they are utterly destroyed.
 And as the slain men fal, the Priests shal trumpet from afar; they
 shal not approach the slain lest they be defiled with unclean blood. For
 they are holy, and they shal not profane the anointing of their
 priesthood with the blood of nations of vanity.
 The Rule for changes in batle order to form the position of a squa[re
 with towers], a concave line with towers, a convex line with towers, a
 sha low convex line obtained by the advance of the centre, or (by the
 advance of) both flanks to terrify the enemy
 The shields of the towers shal be three cubits long and their spears
 eight cubits. The tower shal advance from the formation and shal have
 one hundred shields to each side; in this [manner,] the tower shal be
 surrounded on three sides by three hundred shields. And it shal also
 have two gates, [one to the right] and one to the left.
 They shal write on al the shields of the towers: on the first, Michael,
 [on the second, Gabriel, on the third,] Sariel, and on the fourth,
 Raphael. Michael and Gabriel [shal stand on the right, and Sariel and
 Raphael on the left] ... they shal set an ambush to...
 ... X our camps and to keep us from al that is indecent and evil.
 Furthermore, (Moses) taught us, ‘Thou art in the midst of us, a
 mighty God and terrible, causing al our enemies to flee before [us].’
 He taught our generations in former times saying, When you draw
 near to batle, the Priest sha l rise and speak to the people saying,
 Hear, O Israel! You draw near to batle this day against your
 enemies. Do not fear! Do not let your hearts be afraid! Do not be
 [terrified], and have no fear! For your God goes with you to fight for
 you against your enemies that He may deliver you’ (Deut. XX, 2-4).
 Our officers shal speak to al those prepared for battle. They shal
 strengthen by the power of God the freely devoted of heart, and shal
 make al the fearful of heart withdraw; they shal fortify al the mighty
 men of war. They shal recount that which Thou [saidst] through Moses:
 When you go to war in your land against the oppressor who
 oppresses you, [you] shal blow the trumpets, and you shal be
remembered before your God and shal be saved from your enemies

 O God of Israel, who is like Thee 
in heaven or on earth? 
Who accomplishes deeds and mighty works like Thine? 
Who is like Thy people Israel 
which Thou hast chosen for Thyself 
from al the peoples of the lands; 
the people of the saints of the Covenant, 
instructed in the laws 
and learned in wisdom ... 
who have heard the voice of Majesty 
and have seen the Angels of Holiness, 
whose ear has been unstopped, 
and who have heard profound things?
 [Thou, O God, hast created] the expanse of the heavens 
and the host of heavenly lights, 
the tasks of the spirits 
and the dominion of the Holy Ones, 
the treasury of glory 
[and the canopy of the] clouds. 
(Thou art Creator of) the earth 
and of the laws dividing it into desert and grassland;
 of al that it brings forth 
and of al its fruits [according to their kinds;] 
of the circle of the seas 
and of the gathering-place of the rivers 
and of the divisions of the deeps; 
of the beasts and birds 
and of the shape of Adam 
and of the gene[rations of] his [seed]; 
of the confusion of tongues 
and of the scattering of the peoples, 
of the dweling in clans 
and of the inheritance of lands; 
...of the sacred seasons 
and of the cycles of the years 
and of time everlasting.
 XI Truly the battle is Thine! Their bodies are crushed by the might of
 Thy hand and there is no man to bury them.
 Thou didst deliver Goliath of Gath, the mighty warrior, into the hands
 of David Thy servant, because in place of the sword and in place of the
 spear he put his trust in Thy great Name; for Thine is the battle. Many
 times, by Thy great Name, did he triumph over the Philistines. Many
 times hast Thou also delivered us by the hand of our kings through Thy
 loving-kindness, and not in accordance with our works by which we
 have done evil, nor according to our rebelious deeds.
 Truly the battle is Thine and the power from Thee! It is not ours. Our
 strength and the power of our hands accomplish no mighty deeds
 except by Thy power and by the might of Thy great valour. This Thou
 hast taught us from ancient times, saying, A star sha l come out of
 Jacob, and a sceptre shal rise out of Israel. He shal smite the
 temples of Moab and destroy al the children of Sheth. He shal rule
 out of Jacob and shal cause the survivors of the city to perish. The
 enemy shal be his possession and Israel shal accomplish mighty
 deeds (Num. xxiv, 17-19).
 By the hand of Thine anointed, who discerned Thy testimonies, Thou
 hast revealed to us the [times] of the battles of Thy hands that Thou
 mayest glorify Thyself in our enemies by leveling the hordes of Belial,
 the seven nations of vanity, by the hand of Thy poor whom Thou hast
 redeemed [by Thy might] and by the fulness of Thy marvelous power.
 (Thou hast opened) the door of hope to the melting heart: Thou wilt do
 to them as Thou didst to Pharaoh, and to the captains of his chariots in
 the Red Sea. Thou wilt kindle the downcast of spirit and they shal be a
 flaming torch in the straw to consume ungodliness and never to cease
 ti l iniquity is destroyed.
 From ancient times Thou hast fore[told the hour] when the might of
 Thy hand (would be raised) against the Kittim, saying, Assyria sha l
fa l by the sword of no man, the sword of no mere man shal devour
 him (Isa. xxxi, 8). For Thou wilt deliver into the hands of the poor the
 enemies from al the lands, to humble the mighty of the peoples by the
 hand of those bent to the dust, to bring upon the [head of Thine
 enemies] the reward of the wicked, and to justify Thy true judgement in
 the midst of al the sons of men, and to make for Thyself an everlasting
 Name among the people [whom Thou hast redeemed] ... of battles to
 be magnified and sanctified in the eyes of the remnant of the peoples,
 that they may know... when Thou chastisest Gog and al his assembly
 gathered about him ...
 For Thou wilt fight with them from heaven... X I For the multitude of
 the Holy Ones [is with Thee] in heaven, and the host of the Angels is in
 Thy holy abode, praising Thy Name. And Thou hast established in [a
 community] for Thyself the elect of Thy holy people. [The list] of the
 names of al their host is with Thee in the abode of Thy holiness; [the
 reckoning of the saints] is in Thy glorious dweling-place. Thou hast
 recorded for them, with the graving-tool of life, the favours of [Thy]
 blessings and the Covenant of Thy peace, that Thou mayest reign
 [over them] for ever and ever and throughout al the eternal ages. Thou
 wilt muster the [hosts of] Thine [el]ect, in their Thousands and Myriads,
 with Thy Holy Ones [and with al] Thine Angels, that they may be mighty
 in battle, [and may smite] the rebels of the earth by Thy great
 judgements, and that [they may triumph] together with the elect of
 heaven.
 For Thou art [terrible], O God, in the glory of Thy kingdom, and the
 congregation of Thy Holy Ones is among us for everlasting succour.
 We wil despise kings, we wil mock and scorn the mighty; for our Lord
 is holy, and the King of Glory is with us together with the Holy Ones.
 Valiant [warriors] of the angelic host are among our numbered men,
 and the Hero of war is with our congregation; the host of His spirits is
 with our foot-soldiers and horsemen. [They are as] clouds, as clouds of
 dew (covering) the earth, as a shower of rain shedding judgement on
 al that grows on the earth.
 Rise up, O Hero! 
Lead off Thy captives, O Glorious One!

The War Scrol from Cave 4
 (4Q491, 493)
 Of the two groups of fragments belonging to Ma (4Q491), the first
 echoes sections from columns I, VI, XVI and XVI of 1QM, but it also
 contains passages without paralels there. The second unit, a poem,
 entitled by the editor ‘The Song of Michael and of the Just’, is
 additional to 1QM.
 As for the manuscript designated Mc (4Q493), its surviving lines
 reca l 1QM VI, xvi, etc., but do not represent the same recension.
 Ma = 4Q491, frs. 1-3
 ... There shal be one thousand cubits between the [camp and the
 latrine and] no nakedness [whatever] shal be seen in their
 surroundings. And when they set out to prepare the battle [to cur]b [the
 enemy, there shal be] among them some exempted in the lot of each
 tribe according to their numbered men for [each] day’s duty. On that
 day, some men from al their tribes shal set out from their camps
 towards the House of Meeting ... the [Priest]s, the Levites, and al the
 chiefs of the camps shal go out towards them. They wil pass there
 before ... according to the Thousands, Hundreds, Fifties and Tens.
 Whoever shal not [be clean because of his ‘fount’ on] that [nig]ht shal
 not go with them to the battle, for the holy angels shal be with their
 formations together ... When the formation caled up for that day’s
 battle to pass to al ... of the war, three formations shal stand,
 formations behind formations. They shal set a space between [al] the
 formations [and they shal go out] to battle in succession. These are the
 [foot-soldie]rs and beside them the [horse]men. [They shal stand
between the forma]tions. And if they set up an ambush for a formation,

The Book of War
 (4Q285, 11Q14)
 A colection of ten smal fragments, designated by J. T. Milik as
 Serekh ha-Milhamah or Rule of War, are akin to the War Scrol,
 probably representing its missing end section. A lusions are found to
 Levites blowing trumpets (fr. 8), to the archangel Michael (fr. 10), to the
 Prince of the Congregation, identified as the Branch of David (frs. 4
 and 7), as wel as to the Kittim and their slain (frs. 2, 4, 7), al familiar
 from 1QM. The poorly preserved benediction (fr. 8) can be
 reconstructed with the help of 11Q14 which contains, however, some
 notable variants. Fragment 4 seems to identify the defeated opponent
 as the biblical Gog (Ezek. xxxix), the chief foe of the final age, here no
 doubt equated with the king of the Kittim. Fragment 7, erroneously
 labe led by some as the ‘Pierced Messiah fragment’, is based on an
 interpretation of Isa. x, 34-xi, 1, and should be read in connection with
 4Q161, frs. 8-10, an Isaiah Commentary from Cave 4, and the
 Blessing of the Prince of the Congregation (1QSb, v, 20-29), both
 referring to the triumphant Davidic Messiah, expected to put an
 opponent, no doubt the king of the Kittim, to death. Only frs. 1, 4, 7 and
 8 are suitable for translation.
 For the editioprinceps, see P. S. Alexander and G. Vermes, DJD,
 XXXVI, 228-46. For 11Q14, see F. García Martinez et a/., DJD, XX I,
 243-51. For a preliminary study, see G. Vermes, ‘The Oxford Forum
 for Qumran Research: Seminar on the Rule of War (4Q285)’, JJS 43
 (1992), 85-90.

The Temple Scrol
 (11QT=11Q19-21, 4Q365a, 4Q524)
 Discovered in 1956 in Cave I, the Temple Scrol did not emerge from
 semi-clandestinity until the Six Day War in June 1967. It is the longest
 Qumran manuscript, measuring over twenty-eight feet. There are also
 other fragments pertaining to the same document from Cave 11
 (11Q20) and from Cave 4 (4Q365a). Originaly it consisted of sixty
seven columns.
 The major part of the scrol deals with the Temple (building and
 furniture) and cultic worship, especia ly sacrifices on Sabbaths and the
 many feasts of the year. Most of the legislation depends, directly or
 indirectly, on Exodus, Leviticus, and more particularly on Deuteronomy,
 but there are also occasional non-biblical regulations. The beginning of
 the manuscript is badly mutilated. Column I is missing. Columns I-XI
 are so fragmented that only a very hypothetical reconstruction,
 exclusively from biblical texts, is possible (cf. most extensively E.
 Qimron, The Temple Scrol, 1996). I have decided not to translate
 them but indicate their probable contents in the summary that folows:
 1. Covenant between God and Israel (I).
 2. Building of the Temple, measurements of the Sanctuary, the
 Holy of Holies, the chambers and colonnades ( I-VI).
 3. Description of the mercy seat, the cherubim, the veil, the table,
 the golden lamp-stand, etc. (V I-XI).
 4. Outline of the sacrificesand the altar (XI-X I).
 5. Daily, weekly and monthly sacrifices and those offered on
 festivals (X I-XXIX).
 6. Buildings in the Temple courtyards: the stairhouse, the house of
the laver, the house for sacred vessels, the slaughterhouse, etc.
 (xxx-xxxv).
 7. The three courtyards of the Temple, one for the priests, one for
 Jewish men over twenty years of age, and one for women and
 children (XXXVI-XLV).
 8. Purity regulations concerning the Temple and the city of the
 Sanctuary (XLVI-XLV I).
 9. Purity regulations concerning the cities of Israel (XLV I-LI).
 10. Judges and officers (LI).
 11. Laws relating to idolatry and to sacrificial animals (LI-L I).
 12. Vows and oaths (L I-LIV).
 13. Laws against apostasy (LIV-LV).
 14. Laws relating to priests and Levites and detailed statutes of
 the Jewish king (LVI-LIX).
 15. Miscelaneous laws regarding priestly dues, idols, witnesses,
 the conduct of war, the rebelious son, crimes punishable by
 ‘hanging’, and incestuous relations (LX-LXVI).
 The sequence of subjects generaly folows the Bible, but an obvious
 effort has been made to systematize, harmonize and reinterpret the
 laws. Sections complementary to Scripture include the Temple
 legislation ( I-X I, XXX-XLV), festivals (XV I-XXIX), purity material as
 rules relating to the Temple and the city (XLVI-XLV I), and the statutes
 of the king (LVI—LIX). The aim of the redactor is to present the
 message of the scrol not as an interpretation of the Bible, but as an
 immediate divine revelation. For this purpose, not only does he
 formulate the supplementary legislation as directly spoken by God, but
 also frequently substitutes ‘I’ for ’the Lord = YHWH’ of Scripture.
 Although the view has been advanced that the Temple Scrol is not a
 Qumran composition, the contrary thesis has a solid foundation. The
 relationship between this writing and the Damascus Document is
 particularly striking in the case of the prohibition of royal polygamy, of
 marriage between uncle and niece, and of marital relations within the
 city of the Sanctuary (compare CD IV, 20-V, I; XI, 1-2 with TS LVI, 16
18; LXVI, 15-17; XLV, 11-12), to name the most significant instances.
 Note also that the death penalty of ‘hanging’ (probably crucifixion)
 reserved for traitors appears both in TS LXIV, 6-13 and in the Nahum
Commentary (cf. p. 504). Since the Damascus Document and the
 Nahum Commentary are more likely to depend on the Temple Scrol
 than vice versa, the latter may safely be dated to the second century
 BCE. But it may also have an antecedent history reaching back to the
 pre-Qumran age.
 As noted, Cave 4 has also yielded five fairly mutilated fragments
 (4Q365a), palaeographicaly dated to the mid-first century BCE, some
 of which have been used by Yadin. Fragment 1 deals with the festival
 of Unleavened Bread (11QTS XVI, I); fragment 2 corresponds to
 11QTS XXXV I, 4-15 while the other three fragments cannot be placed
 within the known version of the text.
 The composition is available in a magisterial edition by Yigael
 Yadin, who first published it in Hebrew in 1977 and subsequently,
 shortly before his death, in English under the title The Temple Scro l I
I (Jerusalem, 1983). My translation is often indebted to Yadin’s
 editorial work. Further improvements are due to E. Qimron, The
 Temple Scrol: A Critical Edition with Extensive Reconstructions
 (Beer-Sheva/Jerusalem, 1996). For 11Q20-21, see F. García Martínez
 et al., DJD, XX I, 357-414. For 4Q365a, see S. White, DJD, X I, 319
33. For 4Q524, see E. Puech, DJD, XXV, 79-114.

 horns of the alta[r] of the holocaust and to the four corners of the altar
