 around. He shal burn its fat on the altar, the fat covering the entrails
 and that over the entrails. The appendage of the liver with the kidneys
 he shal remove as wel as the fat over them and on the loins. He shal
 send up in smoke al of them on the altar together with the
 corresponding grain-offering and drink-offering, an offering by fire of
 soothing odour to YHWH. And XXIV ... the flesh, of [soothing] odour; it
shal be [an offering by fire to YHWH. Thus they must do to every]
 young bul, and to every ram and to [every lamb] and its limbs (?) shal
 remain apart. The corresponding [grain-offering] and drink-offering
 shal be on it, an [eternal] rule for your generations before YHWH.
 After this holocaust he shal offer the holocaust of the tribe of Judah
 separately. As he has done with the holocaust of the Levites, so shal
 he do with the holocaust of the children of Judah after the Levites. On
 the second day he shal first offer the holocaust of Benjamin and after it
 he shal offer the holocaust of the children of Joseph, Ephraim and
 Manasseh together. On the third day, he shal offer the holocaust of
 Reuben separately, and the holocaust of Simeon separately. On the
 fourth day he shal offer the holocaust of Issachar separately and the
 holocaust of Zebulun separately. On the fifth day he shal offer the
 holocaust of Gad separately and the holocaust of Asher separately. On
 the sixth day XXV [he shal offer the holocaust of Dan separately and
 the holocaust of Naphtali separately] ...
 In the [seventh] m[onth, on the first day of the month, you shal have] a
 sacred rest, a remembrance announced by a trumpet blast, a [holy]
 ga[thering. You shal offer a holocaust, an offering by fire, of soothing
 odour be]fore YHWH. You shal o[ffer on]e [young bul,] one ram,
 seve[n] ye[ar]ling [lamb]s [without blemish and one he-goat for a sin
offering, and] the corresponding grain-offering and drink-offering
 according to the statute concerning the[m, of soothing odour to YHWH,
 in addition to] the perpetual [holocaus]t [and the holo]caust of the new
 moon. Afterwards [you shal offer] this [holocaust] at the third part of the
 day, an eternal rule for your generation[s wherever you dwel.] You shal
 rejoice on this day. On it you shal do no work. A sacred rest shal this
 day be for you.
 The tenth of this month is the Day of Atonement. You shal mortify
 yourselves. For any person who does not mortify himself on this
 selfsame day shal be cut off from his people. You shal offer on it a
 holocaust to YHWH: one young bul, one ram, seven ram lambs, one
 he-goat for a sin-offering, in addition to the sin-offering of the
 atonement and the corresponding grain-offering and drink-offering
 according to the statute concerning the young bul, the ram, the lambs
 and the he-goat. For the sin-offering of the atonement you shal offer
two rams for holocaust. The High Priest shal offer one for himself and
 his father’s house XXVI ... [The High Prie]st [shal cast lots on the two
 goats,] o[ne] lot for YHWH and one for Azazel. He shal slaughter the
 goat [on] which [YHWH’s lot has falen and shal lift up] its blood in a
 golden bowl which is in [his ha]nd, [and do] with its blo[od as he has
 done with the blood of] his young bul and shal expiate with it for al the
 people of the assembly. He shal send up in smoke its fat and the
 corresponding grain-and drink-offering on the altar of the holocaust. Its
 flesh, skin and dung they shal burn beside his young bul. It is a sin
offering for the whole assembly. He shal expiate with it for al the
 people of the assembly and it shal be forgiven to them. He shal wash
 his hands and feet of the blood of the sin-offering and shal come to the
 living goat and shal confess over its head the iniquities of the children
 of Israel together with al their guilt, a l their sins. He shal put them on
 the head of the goat and despatch it to Azazel in the desert by the
 hand of the man who is waiting ready. The goat shal bear al the
 iniquities of (the children of Israel). XXV I ... [and he sha l expiate] for a l
 the children of Israel and it shal be forgiven to them ... Afterwards he
 shal offer the young bul, the r[a]m, and [the lambs, according to] the
 [sta]tute relating to them, on the altar of the holocaust, and the
 [ho]locaust wi l be accepted for the children of Israel, an eternal rule for
 their generations. Once a year this day shal be for them a memorial.
 They shal do no work on it, for it shal be [to] them a Sabbath of sacred
 rest. Whoever shal do work on it or shal not mortify himself on it, shal
 be cut off from the midst of his people. A Sabbath of sacred rest, a
 holy gathering shal this day be for you. You shal sanctify it as a
 memorial wherever you dwel and you shal do no work.
 On the fifteenth day of this month XXV I ... [the corresponding] grain
offering [and drink-offering, a l on] the altar, an offering by fire, of
 s[oothing odour to YHWH. On] the second [day:] twelve young buls,
 [two rams, four]teen [lambs] and one he-goat [for a sin-offerin]g [and
 the corresponding gr]ai[n-offering and drink-offering] according to the
 statute concerning the young buls, the ram[s], the lambs [and] the he
goat; it is an offering by fire, of soothing odour to YHWH.
 On the third day eleven young buls, two rams, fourteen lambs and
 one he-goat for a sin-offering and the corresponding grain-offering and
drink-offering according to the statute concerning the young buls, the
 rams, the lambs and the he-goat.
 On the fo[ur]th day ten young buls, two rams, fourteen yearling ram
 lambs and one he-goat for a sin-offering and the corresponding grain
offering and drink-offering for the young buls, XXIX [the rams, the
 lambs and the he-goat ... On the fifth day ... and the corresponding
 grain-offering] and drink-offer[ing] ... in the house on which I [shal
 cause] my name to rest ... holocausts, [each on its] day according to
 the law of this statute, always from the children of Israel in addition to
 their freewi lofferings in regard to a l that they offer, their drink-offerings
 and al their gifts that they shal bring to me in order to be acceptable. I
 shal accept them and they shal be my people and I shal be for them
 for ever. I wi l dwel with them for ever and ever and wil sanctify my
 [sa]nctuary by my glory. I wi l cause my glory to rest on it until the day of
 creation on which I shal create my sanctuary, establishing it for myself
 for al time according to the covenant which I have made with Jacob in
 Bethel.
 XXX ... You shal make ... for stairs, a stair[case] ... in the house
 which you shal build ... You [shal make] a staircase north of the
 Temple, a square house, twenty cubits from one corner to the other
 alongside its four corners. Its distance from the wal of the Temple shal
 be seven cubits on the north-west. You shal make the width of its wal
 four cubits ... like the Temple and its inside from corner to corner
 twelv[e cubits.] (There sha l be) a square column in its middle, in the
 centre; its width four cubits on each side around which the stairs wind
 ... XXXI In the upper chamber of [this] ho[use you shal make a ga]te
 opening to the roof of the Temple and a way (shal be) made through
 this gate towards the entrance ... of the Temple by which one can
 reach the upper chamber of the Temple. Overlay with gold [a]l this
 stairhouse, its wals, its gates and its roof, from inside [and from]
 outside, its column and its stairs. [You] sha l do everything as I tel you.
 You shal make a square house for the laver in the south-east, on al its
 sides, (each) twenty-one cubits; fifty cubits distant from the altar. The
 width of the wal shal be four cubits, and the height [t]wenty cubits ...
 Make gates for it on the east, on the north and on the west. The width
 of the gates shal be four cubits and the height seven XXX I ... You shal
make in the wal of this house, on the inside, recesses, and in them ...
 one cubit (in) width and their height four cubits above the ground. They
 shal be overlaid with gold on which they shal place their clothes which
 they have worn on arrival. Above the house of the ... when they come to
 minister in the sanctuary. You shal make a trench around the laver
 beside its house and the trench shal go [from the house of] the laver to
 a cavity. It sha l descend [rapid]ly to the ground where the water shal
 flow and disappear. It shal not be touched by any man for it is mingled
 with the blood of the holocaust. XXX I They shal sanctify my people in
 the sacred vestments which ...
 You shal make a house east of the house of the [l]av[er] according
 to the measurement of [the house of the bas]in. Its wal shal be at a
 distance of seven cubits from the wal of the house of the laver. Its
 whole building and rafters shal be like (those of) the house of the laver.
 It sha l have two gates on the north and the south, one opposite the
 other, according to the measurement of the gates of the house of the
 laver. Inside a l the wals of this house shal have apertures, their width
 (and depth) two cubits each and their height four (?) with which the
 entrails and the feet are raised to the altar. When they have completed
 the sending up in smoke XXXIV ... They close the wheels and ... and
 tie the horns of the young buls to the rings and ... by the rings.
 Afterwards they shal slaughter them and colect [the blood] in bowls
 and toss it around the altar base. They shal open the wheels and strip
 the skin of the young buls from their flesh and cut them up into pieces,
 salt the pieces, wash the entrails and the legs, salt them and send
 them up in smoke on the fire which is on the altar, each young bul with
 its pieces beside it and the corresponding grain-offering of fine flour
 on it, the wine of the drink-offering beside it and some of it on it. The
 priests, the sons of Aaron, shal send everything up in smoke on the
 altar, an offering by fire, of soothing odour before YHWH. You shal
 make chains hanging from the rafters of the twelve columns XXXV ...
 whoever is not a priest shal die, and whoever ... [a prie]st who shal
 come ... and he is not clothed in the [holy] vest[ments in which] he was
 ordained, they too shal be put to death and shal not pro[fane the
 san]ctuary of their God, thus incurring the iniquity of mortal guilt. You
 shal sanctify the environs of the altar, the Temple, the laver and the
colonnade and they shal be most holy for ever and ever.
 You shal make a place west of the Temple, a colonnade of pilars
 standing around for the sin-offerings and the guilt-offerings, divided
 from one another, the sin-offerings of the priests, the he-goats, and the
 sin-offerings of the people and their guilt-offerings. None of these sha l
 be mingled one with another, for their places shal be divided from one
 another in order that the priests may not err concerning al the sin
offerings of the people, and al the rams (?) of the guilt-offerings, (thus)
 incurring the sin of guilt.
 The birds for the altar: he shal prepare turtledoves XXXVI ... from
 the corner of ... [to the corne]r of the gat[e, one hundred and twenty
 cubits.] The gate (shal be) forty [cubits] wide. Each side shal be
 [according to this measurement. The wid]th of [its wa]l shal be seven
 cubits, [and] its [height forty]-five [cubits to the raft]ers of [its] roof. The
 width of its ch[ambers] (shal be) twenty-six cubits from corner to
 corner. The gates of entrance and exit: the gate shal be fourteen
 cubits wide and [tw]enty-eight cubits high from the threshold to the
 lintel. The height of the rafters above the lintel sha l be fourteen cubits.
 (The gate shal be) roofed with a paneling of cedar wood overlaid with
 pure gold. Its doors shal be overlaid with fine gold.
 From the corner of the gate to the second angle of the courtyard,
 (there shal be) one hundred and twenty cubits. Thus shal be the
 measurement of al these gates of the inner courtyard. The gates shal
 lead inside into the courtyard. XXXV I You shal make [in]side the
 court[yard] seats for the priests, and tables in front of the seats, in the
 inner colonnade by the outer wal of the courtyard, places made for the
 priests and their sacrifices, for the firstfruits and the tithes, for their
 peace-offering sacrifices which they shal sacrifice. The sacrifices of
 the peace-offerings of the children of Israel shal not be mingled with
 the sacrifices of the priests.
 In the four corners of the courtyard you shal make for them a place
 for cooking-stoves where they shal seethe their sacrifices [and] sin
offerings. XXXV I ... There they shal eat ... the bird, the turtle-dove and
 the young pigeons ...
 You shal make a second [co]urtyard aro[u]nd [the in]ner [courtyard],
 one hundred cubits wide, and four hundred and eighty cubits long on
the east side, and thus shal be the width and length of al its sides: to
 the south, to the west and to the north. Its wal shal be [fo]ur cubits wide
 and twenty-eight cubits high. Chambers shal be made in the wal
 outside and between each chamber there shal be three-[and-a-half
 cubits] XXXIX ... that al the congregation of the children of Israel may
 bow down before me ... No woman shal come there, nor a child until
 the day that he has fulfi led the rule ... [and has paid for] himself [a
 ransom] to YHWH, half a shekel, an eternal rule, a memorial wherever
 they dwel. The shekel (consists of) twenty gerahs.
 When they shal colect from him the half-shekel... to me. Afterwards
 they shal enter from the age of twenty ... The na[mes of the g]ates of
 this [co]urtyard sha[l b]e according to the nam[es of] the children of
 Is[ra]el: Simeon, Levi and Judah in the east; Reuben, Joseph and
 Benjamin in the south; Issachar, Zebulun and Gad in the west; Dan,
 Naphtali and Asher in the north. Between each gate the measurement
 (sha l be): from the north-eastern corner to the gate of Simeon, ninety
nine cubits, and the gate twenty-eight cubits. From this gate of Simeon
 to the gate of Levi, ninety-nine cubits, and the gate, twenty-eight cubits.
 From the gate of Levi to the gate of Judah XL ... You shal make a third
 courtyard ... to their daughters and to the strangers who [were] born ...
 [wi]de around the middle courtyard ... in length about one thousand six
 [hundred] cubits from one corner to the next. Each side shal be
 according to this measurement: on the east, the south, the west and
 the no[rt]h. The wal shal be seven cubits wide and forty-nine cubits
 high. Chambers shal be made between its gates along the foundation
 as far up as its ‘crowns’ (= crenelations: Yadin). There shal be three
 gates in the east, three in the south, three in the west and three in the
 north. The gates shal be fifty cubits wide and their height seventy
 cubits. Between one gate and another there shal be three hundred
 and sixty cubits. From the corner to the gate of Simeon, three hundred
 and sixty cubits. From the gate of Simeon to the gate of Levi, likewise.
 From the gate of Levi to the gate of Judah, likewise three [hundred
 and] sixty (cubits). XLI ... From the gate of Issachar [to the gate of
 Zebulun, three] hundred [and sixty] cubits. From the gate of Zebulun to
 the gate of Gad, three hundred and sixty cubits. From the ga[te of] Gad
 to the northern corner, three hundred and sixty cubits. From this corner
to the gate of Dan: three hundred and sixty cubits. Thus from the gate
 of Dan to the gate of Naphtali, three hundred and sixty cubits. From the
 gate of Naphtali to the gate of Asher, three hundred and sixty cubits.
 From the gate of Asher to the eastern corner, three hundred and sixty
 cubits. The gates shal jut outwards from the wal of the courtyard seven
 cubits, and extend inwards from the wal to the courtyard thirty-six
 cubits. The entrance of the gate shal be fourteen cubits wide and
 twenty-eight cubits high up to the lintel. The rafters at the doorways (?)
 shal be of cedar wood and overlaid with gold. The doors shal be
 overlaid with pure gold. Between each gate inwards you shal make
 storehouses, XL I [rooms and colonnades.]
 The room shal be ten cubits wide, twenty cubits long, and four[teen]
 cubits high ... with cedar wood. The wal shal be two cubits wide. On
 the outside there shal be storehouses. [The storehouse shal be ten
 cubits wide and] twenty cubits [long]. The wal shal be two cubits wide
 [and fourteen cubits high] up to the lintel. Its entrance shal be three
 cubits wide. [You shal make in this way] al the storehouses and the
 [corresponding] rooms. The colon[nade] ... shal be ten cubits [wi]de.
 Between each gate [you shal make eight]een storehouses and the
 corresponding eight[een] rooms ...
 You shal make a staircase next to the wals of the gates towards the
 colonnade. Winding stairs shal go up to the second and third
 colonnades and to the roof. You shal build storehouses and
 corresponding rooms and colonnades as on the ground floor. The
 second and the third (levels) shal folow the measurement of the lower
 one. On the roof of the third you shal make pilars roofed with rafters
 from one pilar to the next (providing) a place for tabernacles. The
 (pi lars) sha l be eight cubits high and the tabernacles shal be made
 on their (roof) each year at the feast of the Tabernacles for the elders
 of the congregation, for the princes, the heads of the fathers’ houses of
 the children of Israel, the captains of the thousands, the captains of the
 hundreds, who wil ascend and dwel there until the sacrificing of the
 holocaust on the festival which is the feast of the Tabernacles, each
 year. Between each gate there shal be XL I ... on the days of the
 firstfruits of the corn, of the w[ine (tirosh) and the oil, and at the festival
 of the offering of] wood. On these days (the tithe) shal be eaten. They
shal not put aside anything from it from one year to another. For they
 shal eat it in this manner. From the feast of the Firstfruits of the corn of
 wheat they shal eat the corn until the next year, until the feast of the
 Firstfruits, and (they sha l drink) the wine from the day of the festival of
 Wine until the next year, until the day of the festival of the Wine, and
 (they shal eat) the oil from its festival, until the next year, until the
 festival, the day of offering the new oil on the altar. Whatever is left (to
 last beyond) their festivals sha l be sanctified by being burnt with fire. It
 shal no longer be eaten for it is holy. Those who live within a distance
 of three days’ walk from the sanctuary shal bring whatever they can
 bring. If they cannot carry it, they sha l sel it for money and buy with it
 corn, wine, oil, cattle and sheep, and shal eat them on the days of the
 festivals. On working days they shal not eat from this in their weariness
 for it is holy. On the holy days it shal be eaten, but it shal not be eaten
 on working days. XLIV ...
 You shal alot [the rooms and the corresponding chambers. From
 the gate of Simeo]n to the gate of Judah shal be for the priests ... Al
 that is to the right and to the left of the gate of Levi, you shal alo[t] to
 Aaron, your brother, one hundred and eight rooms and corresponding
 chambers and two tabernacles which are on the roof. (You shal alot)
 to the sons of Judah (the area) from the gate of Judah to the corner:
 fifty-four rooms and corresponding chambers and the tabernacle that
 is over them. (You shal alot) to the sons of Simeon (the area) from the
 gate of Simeon to the second corner: their rooms, the corresponding
 chambers and tabernacles. (You shal alot) to the sons of Reuben (the
 area) from the corner which is beside the sons of Judah to the gate of
 Reuben: fifty-two rooms and the corresponding chambers and
 tabernacles. (The area) from the gate of Reuben to the gate of Joseph
 (you shal alot) to the sons of Joseph, to Ephraim and Manasseh. (The
 area) from the gate of Joseph to the gate of Benjamin (you shal alot)
 to the sons of Kohath from the Levites. (The area) from the gate of
 Benjamin to the western corner (you shal alot) to the sons of
 Benjamin. (The area) from this corner to the gate of Issachar (you shal
 alot) to the sons of Issachar. (The area) from the gate (of Issachar)
 XLV . the second (= incoming) [priestly course] shal enter on the left
 ... and the first (= outgoing) shal leave on the right. They shal not
mingle with one another nor their vessels. [Each] priestly course shal
 come to its place and they shal stay there. One shal arrive and the
 other leave on the eighth day. They shal clean the rooms, one after the
 other, when the first (priestly course) leaves. There sha l be no mingling
 there.
 No man who has had a nocturnal emission shal enter the sanctuary
 at al until three days have elapsed. He shal wash his garments and
 bathe on the first day and on the third day he shal wash his garments
 and bathe, and after sunset he shal enter the sanctuary. They shal not
 enter my sanctuary in their impure uncleanness and render it unclean.
 No man who has had sexual intercourse with his wife shal enter
 anywhere into the city of the sanctuary where I cause my name to
 abide, for three days. No blind man shal enter it in al his days and
 shal not profane the city where I abide, for I, YHWH, abide amongst
 the children of Israel for ever and ever.
 Whoever is to purify himself of his flux shal count seven days for his
 purification. He sha l wash his garments on the seventh day and bathe
 his whole body in running water. Afterwards he shal enter the city of
 the sanctuary. No one unclean through contact with a corpse shal enter
 there until he has purified himself. No leper nor any man smitten (in his
 body) shal enter there until he has purified himself and has offered ...
 XLVI ... [No] unclean bird shal fly over [my] sanctua[ry] ... the roofs of
 the gates ... the outer courtyard ... be in my sanctuary for ever and ever
 al the time that I [abide] among them.
 You shal make a terrace round about, outside the outer courtyard,
 fourteen cubits wide like the entrances of al the gates. You shal make
 twelve steps (leading) to it by which the children of Israel shal ascend
 there to enter my sanctuary.
 You shal make a one-hundred-cubits-wide ditch around the
 sanctuary which shal divide the holy sanctuary from the city so that no
 one can rush into my sanctuary and defile it. They shal sanctify my
 sanctuary and hold it in awe because I abide among them.
 You shal make for them latrines outside the city where they shal go
 out, north-west of the city. These shal be roofed houses with holes in
 them into which the filth shal go down. It shal be far enough not to be
 visible from the city, (at) three thousand cubits.
You shal make three areas to the east of the city, divided from one
 another, where the lepers, those suffering from a flux and men who
 have had a (nocturnal) emission XLV I ...
 Their cities [sha l be] pure ... for ever. The city which I wi l sanctify,
 causing my name and [my] sanctuar[y] to abide [in it], shal be holy and
 pure of al impurity with which they can become impure. Whatever is in
 it sha l be pure. Whatever enters it shal be pure: wine, oil, al food and
 al moistened (food) shal be clean. No skin of clean animals
 slaughtered in their cities shal be brought there (to the city of the
 sanctuary). But in their cities they may use them for any work they
 need. But they shal not bring them to the city of my sanctuary, for the
 purity of the skin corresponds to that of the flesh. You shal not profane
 the city where I cause my name and my sanctuary to abide. For it is in
 the skins (of animals) slaughtered in the sanctuary that they shal bring
 their wine and oil and al their food to the city of my sanctuary. They
 shal not polute my sanctuary with the skins of animals slaughtered in
 their country which are tainted (= unfit for the Temple). You cannot
 render any city among your cities as pure as my city, for the purity of
 the skin of the animal corresponds to the purity of its flesh. If you
 slaughter it in my sanctuary, it sha l be pure for my sanctuary, but if you
 slaughter it in your cities, it sha l be pure (only) for your cities. Whatever
 is pure for the sanctuary, shal be brought in skins (fit) for the sanctuary,
 and you shal not profane my sanctuary and my city where I abide with
 tainted skins.
 XLV I ... [the cormorant, the stork, every ki]nd of [heron,] the
 hoop[oe and the bat] ...
 You may eat [the folowing] flying [insects]: every kind of great locust,
 every kind of long-headed locust, every kind of green locust, and every
 kind of desert locust. These are among the flying insects which you
 may eat: those which walk on four legs and have legs jointed above
 their feet to leap with them on the ground and wings to fly with. You
 shal not eat the carcass of any bird or beast but may sel it to a
 foreigner. You shal not eat any abominable thing, for you are a holy
 people to YHWH, your God.
 You are the sons of YHWH, your God. You shal not gash yourselves
 or shave your forelocks in mourning for the dead, nor shal you tattoo
yourselves, for you are a holy people to YHWH, your God. You shal not
 profane your land.
 You shal not do as the nations do; they bury their dead everywhere,
 they bury them even in their houses. Rather you shal set apart areas in
 the midst of your land where you shal bury your dead. Between four
 cities you shal designate an area for burial. In every city you shal set
 aside areas for those stricken with leprosy, with plague and with scab,
 who shal not enter your cities and profane them, and also for those
 who suffer from a flux; and for menstruating women, and women after
 childbirth, so that they may not cause defilement in their midst by their
 impure uncleanness. The leper suffering from chronic leprosy or scab,
 who has been pronounced unclean by the priest XLIX ... with cedar
 wood, hyssop and ... your cities with the plague of leprosy and they
 shal be unclean.
 If a man dies in your cities, the house in which the dead man has
 died shal be unclean for seven days. Whatever is in the house and
 whoever enters the house shal be unclean for seven days. Any food on
 which water has been poured shal be unclean, anything moistened
 shal be unclean. Earthenware vessels shal be unclean and whatever
 they contain shal be unclean for every clean man. The open (vessels)
 shal be unclean for every Israelite (with) whatever is moistened in
 them.
 On the day when the body is removed from there, they shal cleanse
 the house of al polution of oil, wine and water moisture. They shal rub
 its (the house’s) floor, wa ls and doors and shal wash with water the
 bolts, doorposts, thresholds and lintels. On the day when the body is
 removed from there, they shal purify the house and al its utensils,
 hand-mi ls and mortars, al utensils of wood, iron and bronze and al
 utensils capable of purification. Clothes, sacks and skins shal be
 washed. As for the people, whoever has been in the house or has
 entered the house shal bathe in water and shal wash his clothes on
 the first day. On the third day they shal sprinkle purifying water on them
 and shal bathe. They shal wash their garments and al the utensils in
 the house.
 On the seventh day they shal sprinkle (them) a second time. They
 shal bathe, wash their clothes and utensils and shal be clean by the
evening of (the impurity contracted) from the dead so as to (be fit to)
 touch their pure things. As for a man who has not been rendered
 unclean on account of L ... they have been unclean. No longer ... until
 they have sprinkled (them) the second [time] on the seventh day and
 shal be clean by the evening at sunset.
 Whoever touches the bone of a dead person in the fields, or one
 slain by the sword, or a dead body or the blood of a dead person, or a
 tomb, he shal purify himself according to the rule of this statute. But if
 he does not purify himself according to the statute of this law, he is
 unclean, his uncleanness being sti l in him. Whoever touches him must
 wash his clothes, bathe and he shal be clean by the evening.
 If a woman is with child and it dies in her womb, as long as it is dead
 in her, she shal be unclean like a tomb. Any house that she enters shal
 be unclean with al its utensils for seven days. Whoever touches it shal
 be unclean ti l the evening. If anyone enters the house with her, he shal
 be unclean for seven days. He shal wash his clothes and bathe in
 water on the first (day). On the third day he shal sprinkle and wash his
 clothes and bathe. On the seventh day he shal sprinkle a second time
 and wash his clothes and bathe. At sunset he shal be clean.
 As for al the utensils, clothes, skins and al the materials made of
 goat’s hair, you shal deal with them according to the statute of this law.
 Al earthenware vessels shal be broken for they are unclean and can
 no more be purified ever.
 Al creatures that teem on the ground you shal proclaim unclean: the
 weasel, the mouse, every kind of lizard, the wal gecko, the sand
 gecko, the great lizard and the chameleon. Whoever touches them
 dead LI ... [and whatever com]es out of the[m] ... [shal be] unclean [to
 you.] You shal [not] render yourselves unclean by th[em. Whoever
 touches them] dead shal be unclean un[til the] evening. He shal wash
 his clothes and bathe [in water and at] sun[set] he shal be clean.
 Whoever carries any of their bones, their carcass, skin, flesh or claw
 shal wash his clothes and bathe in water. After sunset he shal be
 clean. You shal forewarn the children of Israel about al the impurities.
 They shal not render themselves unclean by those of which I tel you
 on this mountain and they shal not be unclean.
 For I, YHWH, abide among the children of Israel. You shal sanctify
them and they shal be holy. They shal not render themselves
 abominable by anything that I have separated for them as unclean and
 they shal be holy.
 You shal establish judges and officers in al your towns and they
 shal judge the people with just judgement. They shal not be partial in
 (their) judgement. They shal not accept bribes, nor shal they twist
 judgement, for the bribe twists judgement, overturns the works of
 justice, blinds the eyes of the wise, produces great guilt, and profanes
 the house by the iniquity of sin. Justice and justice alone shal you
 pursue that you may live and come to inherit the land that I give you to
 inherit for al days. The man who accepts bribes and twists just
 judgement shal be put to death. You shal not be afraid to execute him.
 You shal not do in your land as the nations do. Everywhere they
 sacrifice, plant sacred trees, erect sacred pi lars and set up carved
 stones to bow down before them and build for them L I ... You shal not
 plant [any tree as a sacred tree beside my altar to be made by you.]
 You shal not erect a sacred pilar [that is hateful to me.] You shal not
 make anywhere in your land a carved stone to bow down before it. You
 shal not sacrifice to me any cattle or sheep with a grave blemish, for
 they are abominable to me. You shal not sacrifice to me any cattle or
 sheep or goat that is pregnant, for this would be an abomination to me.
 You shal not slaughter a cow or a ewe and its young on the same day,
 neither sha l you ki l a mother with her young.
 Of al the firstlings born to your cattle or sheep, you shal sanctify for
 me the male animals. You shal not use the firstling of your cattle for
 work, nor shal you shear the firstling of your smal cattle. You shal eat it
 before me every year in the place that I shal choose. Should it be
 blemished, being lame or blind or (afflicted with) any grave blemish,
 you shal not sacrifice it to me. It is within your towns that you shal eat
 it. The unclean and the clean among you together (may eat it) like a
 gazele or a deer. It is the blood alone that you shal not eat. You shal
 spi l it on the ground like water and cover it with dust. You shal not
 muzzle an ox while it is threshing. You shal not plough with an ox and
 an ass (harnessed) together. You shal not slaughter clean cattle or
 sheep or goat in any of your towns, within a distance of three days’
 journey from my sanctuary. It is rather in my sanctuary that you shal
slaughter it, making of it a holocaust or peace-offering. You shal eat
 and rejoice before me in the place on which I choose to set my name.
 Every clean animal with a blemish, you shal eat it within your towns,
 away from my sanctuary at a distance of thirty stadia. You shal not
 slaughter it close to my sanctuary for its flesh is tainted. You shal not
 eat in my city, which I sanctify by placing my name in it, the flesh of
 cattle, sheep or goat which has not entered my sanctuary. They shal
 sacrifice it there, toss its blood to the base of the altar of holocaust and
 shal burn its fat. L I [When I extend your frontiers as I have told you,
 and if the place where I have chosen to set my name is too distan]t,
 and you say, ‘I wil eat meat’, because you [l]ong for it, [whatever you
 desire,] you may eat, [and you may slau]gh[ter] any of your smal cattle
 or cattle which I give you according to my blessing. You may eat it
 within your towns, the clean and the unclean together, like gazele or
 deer (meat). But you shal firmly abstain from eating the blood. You
 shal spi l it on the ground like water and cover it with dust. For the
 blood is the life and you shal not eat the life with the flesh so that it may
 be wel with you and with your sons after you for ever. You shal do that
 which is correct and good before me, for I am YHWH, your God.
 But al your devoted gifts and votive donations you shal bring when
 you come to the place where I cause my name to abide, and you shal
 sacrifice (them) there before me as you have devoted and vowed them
 with your mouth. When you make a vow, you shal not tarry in fulfi ling it,
 for surely I wi l require it of you and you shal become guilty of a sin. You
 shal keep the word uttered by your lips, for your mouth has vowed
 freely to perform your vow.
 When a man makes a vow to me or swears an oath to take upon
 himself a binding obligation, he must not break his word. Whatever has
 been uttered by his mouth, he shal do it.
 When a woman makes a vow to me, or takes upon herself a binding
 obligation by means of an oath in her father’s house, in her youth, if her
 father hears of her vow or the binding obligation which she has taken
 upon herself and remains silent, al her vows shal stand, and her
 binding obligation which she has taken upon herself shal stand. If,
 however, her father definitely forbids her on the day that he hears of it,
 none of her vows or binding obligations which she has taken upon
herself shal stand, and I wil absolve her because (her father) has
 forbidden her LIV [when he] h[eard of them. But if he annuls them after]
 the da[y that he has] hea[rd of them, he shal bear] her guilt: [her] fa[ther
 has annuled them. Any vow] or binding oath (made by a woman) [to
 mortify herself,] her husband may confi[rm it] or annul it on the day that
 he hears of it, and I wi l absolve her.
 But any vow of a widow or a divorced woman, whatever she has
 taken upon herself shal stand in conformity with al that her mouth has
 uttered.
 Everything that I command you today, see to it that it is kept. You
 shal not add to it, nor detract from it.
 If a prophet or a dreamer appears among you and presents you with
 a sign or a portent, even if the sign or the portent comes true, when he
 says, ‘Let us go and worship other gods whom you have not known!’,
 do not listen to the words of that prophet or that dreamer, for I am
 testing you to discover whether you love YHWH, the God of your
 fathers, with a l your heart and soul. It is YHWH, your God, that you must
 fo low and serve, and it is him that you must fear and his voice that you
 must obey, and you must hold fast to him. That prophet or dreamer
 shal be put to death for he has preached rebelion against YHWH, your
 God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt and redeemed you from
 the house of bondage, to lead you astray from the path that I have
 commanded you to folow. You shal rid yourself of this evil.
 If your brother, the son of your father or the son of your mother, or
 your son, or your daughter, or the wife of your bosom, or your friend
 who is like your own self, (seeks to) entice you secretly, saying, ‘Let us
 go and worship other gods whom you have not known’, neither you, LV
 [nor] your [fa]thers, some of the gods [of the peoples that are round
 about you, whether near you or far off from you], from the one end of
 the earth to [the other, you shal not yield to him or listen to him, nor
 shal your eye pity] him, nor shal you spare [him, nor shal you conceal
 him; but you shal ki l him; your hand shal be first against him to put him
 to death, and afterwards the hand of al the people. You shal stone him
 to death with stones because he sought to] draw you away [from me
 who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage.
 And al Israel shal hear, and fear, and never again do such an evil
thing] among you. If in on[e of your cities in which I] give you to dw[el]
 you hear this said: ‘Men, [s]ons of [Beli]al have arisen in your midst and
 have led astray al the inhabitants of their city saying, “Let us go and
 worship gods whom you have not known!”,’ you shal inquire, search
 and investigate carefuly. If the matter is proven true that such an
 abomination has been done in Israel, you shal surely put al the
 inhabitants of that city to the sword. You shal place it and al who are in
 it under the ban, and you shal put the beasts to the sword. You shal
 assemble al the booty in (the city) square and shal burn it with fire, the
 city and al the booty, as a whole-offering to YHWH, your God. It shal
 be a ruin for ever and shal never be rebuilt. Nothing from that which
 has been placed under the ban shal cleave to your hand so that I may
 turn from my hot anger and show you compassion. I wil be
 compassionate to you and multiply you as I told your fathers, provided
 that you obey my voice, keeping al my commandments that I
 command you today, to do that which is correct and good before
 YHWH, your God.
 If among you, in one of your towns that I give you, there is found a
 man or a woman who does that which is wrong in my eyes by
 transgressing my covenant, and goes and worships other gods, and
 bows down before them, or before the sun or the moon, or al the host
 of heaven, if you are told about it, and you hear about this matter, you
 shal search and investigate it carefuly. If the matter is proven true that
 such an abomination has been done in Israel, you shal lead out that
 man or that woman and stone him (to death) with stones.
 LVI . [You shal go to the Levitical priests o]r to the [j]u[dges then in
 office]; you sha l seek their guidance and [they] shal pro[nounce on] the
 matter for which [you have sought their guidance, and they shal
 procl]aim the(ir) judgement to you. You shal act in conformity with the
 law that they proclaim to you and the saying that they declare to you
 from the book of the Law. They shal issue to you a proclamation in
 truth from the place where I choose to cause my name to abide. Be
 careful to do al that they teach you and act in conformity with the
 decision that they communicate to you. Do not stray from the law which
 they proclaim to you to the right or to the left. The man who does not
 listen but acts arrogantly without obeying the priest who is posted there
to minister before me, or the judge, that man shal die. You shal rid
 Israel of evil. A l the people shal hear of it and shal be awe-stricken,
 and none shal ever again be arrogant in Israel.
 When you enter the land which I give you, take possession of it,
 dwel in it and say, ‘I wi l appoint a king over me as do al the nations
 around me!’, you may surely appoint over you the king whom I wil
 choose. It is from among your brothers that you shal appoint a king
 over you. You shal not appoint over you a foreigner who is not your
 brother. He (the king) shal definitely not acquire many horses, neither
 shal he lead the people back to Egypt for war to acquire many horses
 and much silver and gold, for I told you, ‘You shal never again go back
 that way’. He shal not acquire many wives that they may not turn his
 heart away from me. He shal not acquire very much silver and gold.
 When he sits on the throne of his kingdom, they shal write for him
 this law from the book which is before the priests. LV I This is the law
 [that they sha l write for him] ... [They shal count,] on the day that they
 appoint hi[m] king, the sons of Israel from the age of twenty to sixty
 years according to their standard (units). He shal instal at their head
 captains of thousands, captains of hundreds, captains of fifties and
 captains of tens in al their cities. He shal select from among them one
 thousand by tribe to be with him: twelve thousand warriors who shal
 not leave him alone to be captured by the nations. Al the selected men
 whom he has selected shal be men of truth, God-fearers, haters of
 unjust gain and mighty warriors. They shal be with him always, day and
 night. They shal guard him from anything sinful, and from any foreign
 nation in order not to be captured by them. The twelve princes of his
 people shal be with him, and twelve from among the priests, and from
 among the Levites twelve. They shal sit together with him to (proclaim)
 judgement and the law so that his heart shal not be lifted above them,
 and he shal do nothing without them concerning any affair.
 He shal not marry as wife any daughter of the nations, but shal take
 a wife for himself from his father’s house, from his father’s family. He
 shal not take another wife in addition to her, for she alone shal be with
 him al the time of her life. But if she dies, he may marry another from
 his father’s house, from his family. He shal not twist judgement; he
 shal take no bribe to twist a just judgement and shal not covet a field
or a vineyard, any riches or house, or anything desirable in Israel. He
 shal (not) rob LV I ...
 When the king hears of any nation or people intent on plundering
 whatever belongs to Israel, he shal send for the captains of thousands
 and the captains of hundreds posted in the cities of Israel. They shal
 send with him (the captain) one tenth of the people to go with him (the
 king) to war against their enemies, and they shal go with him. But if a
 large force enters the land of Israel, they shal send with him one fifth of
 the warriors. If a king with chariots and horses and a large force
 (comes), they shal send with him one third of the warriors, and the two
 (remaining) divisions sha l guard their city and their boundaries so that
 no marauders invade their land. If the war presses him (the king) hard,
 they shal send to him half of the people, the men of the army, but the
 (other) half of the people shal not be severed from their cities.
 If they triumph over their enemies, smash them, put them to the
 sword and carry away their booty, they shal give the king his tithe of
 this, the priests one thousandth and the Levites one hundredth from
 everything. They shal halve the rest between the combatants and their
 brothers whom they have left in their cities.
 If he (the king) goes to war against his enemies, one fifth of the
 people shal go with him, the warriors, al the mighty men of valour.
 They shal avoid everything unclean, everything shameful, every iniquity
 and guilt. He shal not go until he has presented himself before the
 High Priest who shal inquire on his behalf for a decision by the Urim
 and Tummim. It is at his word that he shal go and at his word that he
 shal come, he and al the children of Israel who are with him. He shal
 not go folowing his heart’s counsel until he (the High Priest) has
 inquired for a decision by the Urim and Tummim. He shal (then)
 succeed in al his ways on which he has set out according to the
 decision which LIX ... and they shal disperse them in many lands and
 they shal become a h[orror], a byword, a mockery. With a heavy yoke
 and in extreme want, they shal there serve gods made by human
 hands, of wood and stone, silver and gold. During this time their cities
 shal become a devastation, a laughing-stock and a wasteland, and
 their enemies shal devastate them. They shal sigh in the lands of their
 enemies and scream because of the heavy yoke. They shal cry out but
I wi l not listen; they sha l scream but I wi l not answer them because of
 their evil doings. I wi l hide my face from them and they shal become
 food, plunder and prey. None shal save them because of their
 wickedness, because they have broken my covenant and their soul
 has loathed my law until they have incurred every guilt. Afterwards they
 wi l return to me with al their heart and al their soul, in conformity with
 al the words of this law, and I wil save them from the hand of their
 enemies and redeem them from the hand of those who hate them, and
 I wi l bring them to the land of their fathers. I wi l redeem them, and
 increase them and exult over them. I wi l be their God and they shal be
 my people.
 The king whose heart and eyes have gone astray from my
 commandments shal never have one to sit on the throne of his fathers,
 for I wi l cut off his posterity for ever so that it shal no more rule over
 Israel. But if he walk after my rules and keep my commandments and
 do that which is correct and good before me, no heir to the throne of
 the kingdom of Israel shal be cut off from among his sons for ever. I wi l
 be with him and wil save him from the hand of those who hate him and
 from the hand of those who seek his life. I wil place al his enemies
 before him and he shal rule over them according to his pleasure and
 they shal not rule over him. I wil set him on an upward, not on a
 downward, course, to be the head and not the tail, that the days of his
 kingdom may be lengthened greatly for him and his sons after him.
 LX ... and al their wave-offerings. A l their firstling male [bea]sts and
 al ... of their beasts and al their holy gifts which they shal sanctify to
 me together with al their holy gifts of praise and a proportion of their
 offering of birds, wild animals and fish, one thousandth of their catch,
 and al that they shal devote, and the proportion of the booty and the
 plunder.
 To the Levites shal belong the tithe of the corn, the wine and the oil
 that they have sanctified to me first; the shoulder from those who
 slaughter a sacrifice and a proportion of the booty, the plunder and the
 catch of birds, wild animals and fish, one hundredth; the tithe from the
 young pigeons and from the honey one fiftieth. To the priests shal
 belong one hundredth of the young pigeons, for I have chosen them
 from al your tribes to attend on me and minister (before me) and bless
my name, he and his sons always. If a Levite come from any town
 anywhere in Israel where he sojourns to the place where I wi l choose
 to cause my name to abide, (if he come) with an eager soul, he may
 minister like his brethren the Levites who attend on me there. He shal
 have the same share of food with them, besides the inheritance from
 his father’s family.
 When you enter the land which I give you, do not learn to practise the
 abominations of those nations. There shal be found among you none
 who makes his son or daughter pass through fire, nor an augur or a
 soothsayer, a diviner or a sorcerer, one who casts spels or a medium,
 or wizards or necromancers. For they are an abomination before me,
 al who practise such things, and it is because of these abominations
 that I drive them out before you. You shal be perfect towards YHWH,
 your God. For these nations that LXI ... to ut[ter a word] in [my] n[ame
 which I have n]ot comman[ded him to] utter, or wh[o speaks in the
 name of oth]er go[ds], that prophet shal be put to death. If you say in
 your heart, ‘How shal we know the word which YHWH has not
 uttered?’, when the word uttered by the prophet in the name of YHWH
 is not fulfi led and does not come true, that is not a word that I have
 uttered. The prophet has spoken arrogantly; do not fear him.
 A single witness may not come forward against a man in the matter
 of any iniquity or sin which he has committed. It is on the evidence of
 two witnesses or three witnesses that a case can be established. If a
 malicious witness comes forward against a man to testify against him
 in a case of a crime, both disputants shal stand before me and before
 the priests and the Levites and before the judges then in office, and the
 judges shal inquire, and if the witness is a false witness who has
 testified falsely against his brother, you sha l do to him as he proposed
 to do to his brother. You shal rid yourselves of evil. The rest shal hear
 of it and shal be awe-stricken and never again shal such a thing be
 done in your midst. You shal have no mercy on him: life for life, eye for
 eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.
 When you go to war against your enemies, and you see horses and
 chariots and an army greater than yours, be not afraid of them, for I am
 with you who brought you out of the land of Egypt. When you approach
 the battle, the priest sha l come forward to speak to the army and say
to them, ‘Hear, Israel, you approach ... ’ ... LX I [and another man shal
 use its fruit. If any man has betrothed a woman but has not yet married
 her, he shal return] home. Otherwise he may die in the war and
 another man may take her. [The] of[ficers shal continue] to address the
 army and say, ‘If any man is afraid and has lost heart, he shal go and
 return. Otherwise he may render his kinsmen as faint-hearted as
 himself.’
 When the judges have finished addressing the army, they shal
 appoint army captains at the head of the people.
 When you approach a city to fight it, (first) offer it peace. If it seeks
 peace and opens (its gates) to you, then al the people found in it shal
 become your forced labourers and shal serve you. If it does not make
 peace with you, but is ready to fight a war against you, you shal
 besiege it and I wi l deliver it into your hands. You shal put al its males
 to the sword, but the women, the children, the beasts and al that is in
 the city, a l its booty, you may take as spoil for yourselves. You may
 enjoy the use of the booty of your enemies which I give you. Thus shal
 you treat the very distant cities, those which are not among the cities of
 these nations. But in the cities of the peoples which I give you as an
 inheritance, you shal not leave alive any creature. Indeed you shal
 utterly exterminate the Hittites, the Amorites, the Canaanites, the
 Hivites, the Jebusites, the Girgashites and the Perizzites as I have
 commanded you, that they may not teach you to practise al the
 abominations that they have performed to their gods.
 LX I ... [a heifer with which] he has not worked, which [has not drawn
 the yoke. The elders of] that city [shal bring down] the heifer to a ravine
 with an ever-flowing stream which has never been sown or cultivated,
 and there they shal break its neck.
 The priests, the sons of Levi, shal come forward, for I have chosen
 them to minister before me and bless my name, and every dispute and
 every assault shal be decided by their word. Al the elders of the city
 nearest to the body of the murdered man shal wash their hands over
 the head of the heifer whose neck has been broken in the ravine. They
 shal declare, ‘Our hands did not shed this blood, nor did our eyes see
 it happen. Accept expiation for thy people Israel whom thou hast
 redeemed, O YHWH, and do not permit the guilt of innocent blood to
rest among thy people, Israel. Let this blood be expiated for them.’ You
 shal rid Israel (of the guilt) of innocent blood, and you shal do that
 which is correct and good before YHWH, your God. When you go to
 war against your enemies, and I deliver them into your hands, and you
 capture some of them, if you see among the captives a pretty woman
 and desire her, you may take her to be your wife. You shal bring her to
 your house, you shal shave her head, and cut her nails. You shal
 discard the clothes of her captivity and she shal dwel in your house,
 and bewail her father and mother for a ful month. Afterwards you may
 go to her, consummate the marriage with her and she wil be your wife.
 But she shal not touch whatever is pure for you for seven years, neither
 shal she eat of the sacrifice of peace-offering until seven years have
 elapsed. Afterwards she may eat. LXIV ... [the firstfruits of his virility;
 he has the right of the first-born.]
 If a man has a disobedient and rebelious son who refuses to listen
 to his father and mother, nor listens to them when they chastise him,
 his father and mother shal take hold of him and bring him to the elders
 of his city, to the gate of his place. They shal say to the elders of his
 town, ‘This son of ours is disobedient and rebelious; he does not listen
 to us; he is a glutton and a drunkard.’ Al the men of his city shal stone
 him with stones and he shal die, and you shal rid yourselves of evil. Al
 the children of Israel shal hear of it and be awe-stricken. If a man
 slanders his people and delivers his people to a foreign nation and
 does evil to his people, you shal hang him on a tree and he shal die.
 On the testimony of two witnesses and on the testimony of three
 witnesses he shal be put to death and they shal hang him on the tree.
 If a man is guilty of a capital crime and flees (abroad) to the nations,
 and curses his people, the children of Israel, you shal hang him also on
 the tree, and he shal die. But his body shal not stay overnight on the
 tree. Indeed you shal bury him on the same day. For he who is hanged
 on the tree is accursed of God and men. You shal not polute the
 ground which I give you to inherit. If you see your kinsman’s ox or
 sheep or donkey straying, do not neglect them; you shal indeed return
 them to your kinsman. If your kinsman does not live near you, and you
 do not know who he is, you shal bring the animal to your house and it
 shal be with you until he claims (it). LXV ...
[Wh]en a bird’s nest happens to lie before you by the roadside, on
 any tree or on the ground, with fledglings or eggs, and the hen is sitting
 on the fledglings or the eggs, you shal not take the hen with the young.
 You shal surely let the hen escape and take only the young so that it
 may be wel with you and your days shal be prolonged. When you build
 a new house, you shal construct a parapet on the roof so that you do
 not bring blood-guilt on your house if anyone should fal from it.
 When a man takes a wife, has sexual intercourse with her and takes
 a dislike to her, and brings a baseless charge against her, ruining her
 reputation, and says, ‘I have taken this woman, approached her, and
 did not find the proof of virginity in her’, the father or the mother of the
 girl sha l take the girl’s proof of virginity and bring it to the elders at the
 gate. The girl’s father sha l say to the elders, ‘I gave my daughter to be
 this man’s wife; he has taken a dislike to her and has brought a
 baseless charge against her saying, “I have not found the proof of
 virginity in your daughter.” Here is the proof of my daughter’s virginity.’
 They shal spread out the garment before the elders of that city. The
 elders of that city shal take that man and chastise him. They shal fine
 him one hundred pieces of silver which they shal give to the father of
 the girl, because he (the husband) has tried to ruin the reputation of an
 Israelite virgin. He sha l not LXVI ... [When a virgin betrothed to a man
 is found by another man in the city and he lies with her, they shal bring
 both of them to the gate] of that city and stone them with stones and
 they shal be put to death: the girl because she has not shouted (for
 help, although she was) in the city, and the man because he has
 dishonoured his neighbour’s wife. You shal rid yourselves of evil. If the
 man has found the woman in the fields in a distant place hidden from
 the city, and raped her, only he who has lain with her shal be put to
 death. To the girl they shal do nothing since she has committed no
 crime worthy of death. For this affair is like that of a man who attacks
 his neighbour and murders him. For it was in the fields that he found
 her and the betrothed girl shouted (for help), but none came to her
 rescue.
 When a man seduces a virgin who is not betrothed, but is suitable to
 him according to the rule, and lies with her, and he is found out, he who
 has lain with her shal give the girl’s father fifty pieces of silver and she
shal be his wife. Because he has dishonoured her, he may not divorce
 her al his days. A man shal not take his father’s wife and shal not lift
 his father’s skirt. A man shal not take the wife of his brother and shal
 not lift the skirt of his brother, the son of his father or the son of his
 mother, for this is unclean. A man shal not take his sister, the daughter
 of his father or the daughter of his mother, for this is abominable. A
 man shal not take his father’s sister or his mother’s sister, for this is
 immoral. A man shal not take the daughter of his brother or the
 daughter of his sister for this is abominable. (A man) shal not take
 LXVI ...
MMT (Miqsat Ma‘ase Ha- Torah) - Some
 Observances of the Law
 (4Q394-9)
 Mutilated fragments of six Cave 4 manuscripts have acquired
 international notoriety on account of a lawsuit filed before the
 Jerusalem district court which found that one of the official editors of
 this text, Elisha Qimron, owned the copyright of the MMT text as
 reconstructed by him. Hershel Shanks, of the Biblical Archaeology
 Society of Washington, was held responsible for breaching this
 copyright and ordered to pay $43,000 damages. However, an appeal
 having been lodged with the Israeli Supreme Court, the matter is sti l
 subjudice.
 When the contents of the six copies are assembled into a single
 account, they amount to 120 lines mostly of a fragmentary nature. The
 document begins with a sectarian calendar (section A), which may or
 may not pertain to the original MMT; it continues with a series of
 special rules (section B), and ends with an exhortation (section C).
 MMT has been caled an epistle, but since it lacks the introductory and
 concluding formulae of a letter, it is more likely to be a kind of legal
 tractate. It is addressed to a single leading personality who is
 compared to King David. The dramatis personae consist of a ‘we’
 party, a ‘you’ party, and a ‘they’ party. The group responsible for MMT,
 who refer to themselves as ‘we‘, seek to detach the leader of the ‘you’
 party from the erroneous views propounded by the ‘they’ party. The
 editors claim that MMT was written by the Teacher of Righteousness
 and sent to the Wicked Priest; that the views of the ‘we’ party are akin
 to those of the Sadducees; and that the ‘they’ party are the Pharisees.
 However, these are no more than hypotheses. In particular, the
 identification of the author with the Teacher of Righteousness is less
 likely than that of the leader of the ‘you’ group with the man who was to
become the Wicked Priest, probably Jonathan Maccabaeus.
 It has also been advanced that the original nucleus of the Qumran
 Community, responsible for MMT, consisted of early or proto
Sadducees. However, one should bear in mind that the priests in this
 writing are never caled sons of Zadok or Sadducees, but are referred
 to as sons of Aaron (cf. 4QSb (=4Q256) and 4QSd (4Q258) above, p.
 118). The aleged Sadducee connection relies on three legal rules, out
 of a list of more than twenty, in MMT which represent opinions identical
 with the halakhah attributed in rabbinic literature to the Sadducees,
 and contrasted with the stance taken by the Pharisees (cf. mParah 5:4
 and MMT B 13-17; mYad 4:7; mToh 8:9 and MMT B 55-8; mYad 4:6
 and MMT B 72-4).
 The chief topics of controversy are (I) the calendar (if it is an integral
 part of the document); (2) ritual purity (acceptability of Gentile offerings,
 law on slaughter, the ‘red heifer’ ritual, exclusion of the blind and the
 deaf, law relating to lepers, purity of running liquids, fourth-year fruit
 and tithe of cattle, ban on dogs in Jerusalem, the law regulating contact
 with dead bodies); (3) marriage and intermarriage rules, and (4)
 decrees regulating entry into the congregation. MMT is particularly
 important as a source of ancient legal debate. It is unique among the
 Dead Sea Scrols and foreshadows the halakhic process developed
 and practised by later rabbis.
 Instead of presenting a composite text, the translation wi l reflect
 actual manuscripts and folow their line numbering. If there are
 overlapping fragments, gaps in the main manuscript wi l be fi led from
 the paralel text and the borrowing wi l appear between {}. However, to
 assist the reader who desires to consult the Qimron-Strugnel edition,
 the line numbers of their composite text are given in brackets. Unlike
 the editors, I have been economical with purely conjectural restorations
 of the many gaps. Obscurities in the translation therefore faithfuly
 reflect the real status of the Hebrew original.
 For the editio princeps, see E. Qimron and J. Strugnel, Qumran
 Cave 4, V (DJD, X, Clarendon Press, Oxford, 1994).

The Wicked and the Holy
 (4Q181)
 The first fragment of a document from Cave 4(4Q181), which its editor
 has left untitled, describes in a manner similar to Community Rule IV
 the respective destinies of the damned and the chosen. See J. M.
 Alegro and A. A. Anderson, DJD,V, 79-80; cf. J. Strugnel, RQ 7
 (1970), 254-5; J. T. Milik, JJS 23 (1972), 114-18.
 ... for guilt with the congregation of his people, for it has walowed in
 the sin of the sons of men; (and it was appointed) for great judgements
 and evil diseases in the flesh according to the mighty deeds of God
 and in accordance with their wickedness. In conformity with their
 congregation of uncleanness, (they are to be separated) as a
 community of wickedness until (wickedness) ends.
 In accordance with the mercies of God, according to His goodness
 and wonderful glory, He caused some of the sons of the world to draw
 near (Him) ... to be counted with Him in the com[munity of the ‘g]ods’
 as a congregation of holiness in service for eternal life and (sharing)
 the lot of His holy ones ... each man according to his lot which He has
 cast for ... for eternal life ...
4QHalakhah A
 (4Q251)
 Twenty-six severely damaged fragments, palaeographicaly dated to
 the late first century BCE, represent extracts from the Pentateuch with
 textual variations and exegetical paraphrases. The main topics are the
 Sabbath, compensation for injuries, firstfruits, the priest’s wife,
 forbidden marital unions, etc.
 For the editio princeps, see E. Larson, M. R. Lehman and L.
 Schiffman, DJD, XXXV, 25-51.
 Frs. 1-2

 into it] or from the house outsi[de] ... for him to interpret and read in the
 Book on the [Sabba]th ...
 Frs. 4-7 i

 his fist and the man does not] di[e but keeps] his [be]d, [then if the man
 rises again] and w[alks outside, he who injur]ed him is not guilty. Only
 [he shal pay for the loss of his time] and for his medical [treatment].

4QHalakhah B
 (4Q264a)
 Three fragments of a legal document, the first of which partly overlaps
 with various sections of 4Q421, deal with Sabbath regulations.
 For the editio princeps, see J. M. Baumgarten, DJD, XXXV, 53-6.
 Fr. I i
 I ... [No man shal revise the scro]l of a book reading its script on the
 day of [Sabbath] ... But they may read and study it. No man shal make
 plans with his mouth ... [on the day of Sabbath. He shal not talk] about
 any matter relating to work or wealth or ... on the day of Sabbath. He
 shal sp[eak no wo]rd apart from speaking holy words as prescribed
 and from pronouncing blessings of God. He may talk about eating and
 drink[ing] ...
4QTohorot (Purities) A
 (4Q274)
 This is the first of ten Cave 4 manuscripts dealing with purity matters.
 The text translated represents column I of the document and the first
 word of column I. 4Q274 deals with uncleanness caused by bodily
 fluxes and issues of blood and with the means of its removal. Parts of
 fr. 3 are concerned with the uncleanness associated with the juice
 oozing out of fruit.
 For the editio princeps, see J. M. Baumgarten, DJD, XXXV, 99
109.
 Fr. I i
 I ... [Let him not] begin to cast his lot. He shal lie on a bed of sorrow
 and sit in a seat of sighs. He shal dwel in isolation with al the unclean,
 and away from (food) purity at a distance of twelve cubits in the wing
 (assigned) to him (?) on the north-west of every dweling-house
 according to this measure. Every man from among the unclean ... he
 shal bathe in water [on the seven]th d[ay] and wash his clothes, and
 afterwards he may eat. For this is what he said, He sha l cry, Unclean,
 unclean (Lev. xiii, 45), as long as [the pla]gue af[fects him].
 A woman with a seven-day issue of blood shal not touch a man with
 a flux, nor any vessel touched by a man who has a flux, nor anything he
 has lain or sat on. But if she has touched (them), she shal wash her
 garments and bathe, and afterwards she may eat. Above al, she shal
 not mingle (with the pure) [during] her seven days so that she may not
 polute the c[amp]s of the Ho[ly] of Israel. Neither shal she touch any
 woman with a long-term issue of blood. And the person, either male or
female, who counts (the seven days: cf. Lev. xv, 13) shal not touch the
 menstruant during her uncleanness. Only when she has purified herself
 [from] her [un]cleanness (may she be touched), for the blood of the
 menstruant is reckoned as a flux for anyone who touches it. And if he
 has touched [bodily] f[lux or s]emen, h[e shal] be unclean. [And he who
 has tou]ched a man from among al these unclean persons during the
 seven days of [his] cleans[ing] he shal [n]ot eat. If he has become
 unclean because of a corp[se, he shal bathe in wat]er, wash (his
 garments) and afterwards I he may e[at].
 Fr. 2 i

 (his clothes) before [he eats. If it oc]curs for him on the seventh (day)
 on the Sabbath day, he shal not sprinkle on the Sabbath, for [He says:
 Keep] the Sabbath (Deut. v, 12). But he shal not touch anything pure
 until he has repeated it. Al that touches semen be it a man or any
 vessel shal be immersed. And the bearer of it [shal immer]se. And the
 garment on which it (the semen) is and the vessel which carries it shal
 be immersed [in wate]r. If there is a man in the camp who is unable to
 do this (lacks a spare garment), he shal bath[e and put on an]y

 does not touch his bread (food). He who touches [his bed or] his [sea]t,

 water. But if [his garment has touched it (the bed or the seat)], he shal

4QTohorot Ba-Bb
 (40276-7)
 These two fragments deal with the biblical law of the ‘red heifer’, the
 ashes of which were used for the preparation of the ‘water for (the
 removal of) uncleanness’ necessary for the cleansing of impurity
 resulting from contact with a dead body. Relevant extracts from Num.
 xix are freely quoted. The subject is treated also in MMT B 13 (4Q394
 frs. 3-7 i, 16-20). The script dates to the late first century BCE.
 For the editio princeps, see J. M. Baumgarten, DJD, XXXV, 111
19.
 4Q276
 [And the priest wears the garments] in which he is not ministering in
 the Sanctuary ... renders the garments guilty. And he slaughtered [the]
 heifer before Him. He shal carry her blood in a clay vessel which is
 [not brough]t near the altar. And with his finger he shal sprinkle some
 of her blood seven [times towa]rds the front of the tent of meeting. And

4Q Harvesting
 (4Q284a)
 Four fragments of a document have survived in a late Hasmonaean—
 early Herodian script (mid-first century BCE). They deal with matters of
 uncleanness affecting fruits. Only fr. 1 is translatable. The phrase
 ‘liquids of the Congregation’ links this fragment to IQS VI, 20, V I, 20.
 For the editio princeps, see J. M. Baumgarten, DJD, XXXV, 131-3.

 [and the pomegranates, if] their ju[ice] oozes out wh[en he squee]zes

 gathered them. And if they press [olives in the olive press], let him not

 the press].... in purity ...
The Master’s Exhortation to the Sons of Dawn
 (40298)
 Eight fragments of a manuscript which, apart from its title, is written in
 a cryptic alphabet, contain an exhortation to a group, designated as
 ‘sons of dawn’ by the ‘Master’ (maskil), the title of the teacher in
 charge of instruction in the Community (cf. Community Rule). The
 phrase, ‘sons of dawn’ (bene ha-shahar) is possibly attested in the
 Damascus Document X I, 14 (cf. M. Broshi, The Damascus
 Document Reconsidered, Jerusalem, 1992, [35]: ‘No member of the
 Covenant shal have any dealings with the sons of dawn except for
 payment’). The earlier reading was ‘sons of the Pit’ (bene ha-shahat).
 S. Pfann suggests that ‘the sons of dawn’ (not yet ‘sons of light’) are
 newcomers to the sect at the earliest stages of their initiation. The
 exhortation reca ls the opening pages of the Damascus Document.
 The square script of the title is said to belong to the second half of the
 first century BCE. Parts of frs. 1-2 and 3-4 are large enough to be
 translated.
 For the editio princeps, see Stephen Pfann and M. Kister, DJD, XX,
 1-30.
 4Q298, frs. 1-2 i
 [Wor]d of the Master which he spoke to al the sons of Dawn. Liste[n to
 me a]l men of heart (=inteligence) and understand my word. [And
 seeke]rs of righteousness, h[ea]r my word in al that proceeds from
 [my] lips. Those who [k]now have sear[ch]ed [th]ese (matters) and
 [have] returned [to the path] of life ...
Frs- 3-4 i

 And men of understanding, in[crease migh]t, and modesty, you who

 end of the ages and you wil gaze at ancient things to know ...

Register of Rebukes
 (4Q477)
 Fragments of two columns of a document contain a list of Community
 members rebuked for offences against the rules. This is the only scrol
 fragment which reveals the names of individual members: Yohanan
 son of Ar[ ], Hananiah Notos and Hananiah son of Sim[on]. According
 to the editor, Esther Eshel, the rebukes listed here were read out in
 public by the mebaqqer (or Guardian), hence the title given by her, The
 Rebukes Reported by the Overseer. To be more precise, it is likely
 that the rebukes originated with witnesses of the offence. They
 reported it to the Guardian who was to record the infringement (cf. CD
 IX, 2-4; 16-20). The epithet Notos attached to the name of Hananiah
 probably means ‘Southerner’ in line with a paralel Masada inscription
 (no. 462: Shim’on bar Notos) according to Y. Yadin and J. Naveh,
 Masada I (Jerusalem, 1989), 40. D. Flusser, as quoted by Eshel,
 associates Notos with the Greek nothos (bastard). In col. I, 1. 8, Eshel,
 fo lowing M. Broshi, reads shyr (=sh’r) bsrw (i.e. near kin); R. Eisenman
 and M. Wise have shpk bsrw (emission of his body) and J. M.
 Baumgarten (JJS 45 (1994), 277) swd bsrw (the carnal foundation of
 man).
 Frs. 1, 2 i and 3: the text is too fragmentary for translation. Note the
 significant phrases ‘[to] recal their transgression’, ‘men of the
 [Community?]’, ‘to rebuke’ and [‘c]amps of the Congregation’ and ‘they
 rebuked’.
 For the editio princeps, see E. Eshel, DJD, XXXVI, 474-83.
 Fr. 2
I ... who ... [wh]o acted wickedly ... the Congregation ... Yohanan son of
 Ar ... [they rebuked because] he was short-tempered ... with him ... the
 iniquity with him and also the spirit of pride was with [him] ... vacat
 They rebuked Hananiah Notos because he ... [to dis]turb the spirit of
 the Communi[ty ... and] also to mingle the ... they rebu[k]ed because
 evil ... was with him and also because he was not ... and also because
 he loved his bodily nature (or: showed preference to his near kin) ...
 [blank] And [they rebuked] Hananiah son of Sim[on] [because he] ...
 and he also loves the goodness ...
Remonstrances (before Conversion?)
 (4Q471a)
 This smal fragment, written in Herodian script, contains reproofs
 addressed in the second person plural to a group of wicked Jews. The
 context is that of a war. It is unlikely to belong to the War Scrol or the
 Book of War as neither of these includes speeches to outsiders.
 According to a conjecture proposed by the editors, the opponents of
 the sect thus criticized are the ruling class of Judaea (Hasmonaeans
 and perhaps also Sadducees), but nothing in the surviving text
 positively supports their cautiously presented surmise. However, the
 second half of the fragment can be interpreted in a positive sense, in
 which case the scene may be a last-minute mass conversion of
 unfaithful Jews before the final battle.
 For the editio princeps, see Esther Eshel and Menahem Kister,
 DJD, XXXVI, 446-9.

 His covenant ... [You] said: Let us fight His wars for He has redeemed
 us ... Your [mighty men] shal be humbled. And they did not know that
 He has despised ... you shal show yourselves mighty in war. And you
 have been reckoned ... by His measuring line(?). You shal seek
 righteous judgement and the work ... you shal exalt yourselves. And He
 has chosen t[hem] ... for a cry ... And you wi l return ... sweet
B. Hymns and Poems
 ‘Thanksgiving Scro l’, 
The Shrine of the Book, 
Israel Museum, Jerusalem
The Thanksgiving Hymns
 (IQH, IQ36, 4Q427-32)
 The Hymns Scrol was published by E. L. Sukenik in 1954-5 (The
 Dead Sea Scrols of the Hebrew University, Jerusalem). It has
 suffered a good deal of deterioration and the translator has difficulty,
 not only in making sense of the poems, but also in determining where
 one ends and another begins. For a method of restoration of the
 Hymns, see E. Puech, ‘Quelques aspects de la restauration du
 Rouleau des Hymnes’, JJS 39 (1988), 38-55. He has convincingly
 argued that apart from some fragments, the first three columns of the
 original Hymns Scrol are lost. The missing beginning should be
 fo lowed by the existing columns in the folowing order: XVI (sheet 1),
 X I-XVI (sheet 2), I-IV (sheet 3), V-V I (sheet 4) and IX-X I of the editio
 princeps. Four further columns (XXI-XXIV) may be reconstructed with
 the help of the former col. XV I and various fragments published by
 Sukenik. Puech tentatively suggests also two further columns (XXV
XXVI) made up from other fragments.
 Our presentation of the Hymns is now rearranged according to
 Puech’s thesis and his reconstruction of the poem in col. VI (formerly
 XIV) is also adopted (cf. art. cit., 53-4). No translation is offered for the
 extremely fragmentary cols. XXIV-XXVI, though it should be noted that
 col. xxv includes the beginning of a new poem, ‘For the Master. A
 s[ong (?)]’ from IQH, fr. 8. Further fragments, occasionaly used to
 improve the reading of IQH, may be found at IQ35 (cf. also IQ36 and
 37-40; see DJD, I, 136-43) and 4Q427-32; for the latter, see Eileen
 Schuler, DJD, XXIX, 69-232.
 The poems contained in the Scrol are similar to the biblical Psalms.
 They are mostly hymns of thanksgiving, individual prayers as opposed
 to those intended for communal worship, expressing a rich variety of
 spiritual and doctrinal detail. But the two fundamental themes running
through the whole colection are those of salvation and knowledge. The
 sectary thanks God continualy for having been saved from the ‘lot’ of
 the wicked, and for his gift of insight into the divine mysteries. He, a
 ‘creature of clay’, has been singled out by his Maker to receive favours
 of which he feels himself unworthy and he aludes again and again to
 his frailty and total dependence on God. Whereas some of the Hymns
 give expression to thoughts and sentiments common to al the
 members of the sect, others, particularly nos. 1, 2, and 7-11, appear to
 refer to the experiences of a teacher abandoned by his friends and
 persecuted by his enemies. Several scholars tend to ascribe the
 authorship of these to the Teacher of Righteousness, and even
 consider that he may be responsible for al the Hymns. But although
 this hypothesis is not impossible, no sure conclusion can yet be
 reached. Nor are we in a position to date any particular composition.
 The most we can say is that the colection as such probably attained its
 final shape during the last pre-Christian century.
 Philo’s account of the banquet celebrated by the contemplative
 Essenes, or Therapeutae, on the Feast of Pentecost may indicate the
 use to which the Hymns were put. He reports that when the President
 of the meeting had ended his commentary on the Scriptures, he rose
 and chanted a hymn, either one of his own making or an old one, and
 after him each of his brethren did likewise (The Contemplative Life, §
 80). Similarly, it is probable that the psalms of this Scrol were recited
 by the Guardian and newly initiated members at the Feast of the
 Renewal of the Covenant. Hymn 4 (formerly 21) expressly refers to the
 oath of the Covenant, and Hymn 5 (formerly 22) appears to be a poetic
 commentary on the liturgy marking the entry into the Community.
 Indeed, the relative poverty of principal themes may be due to the fact
 that al this poetry was intended for a special occasion and its
 inspirational scope was thereby limited.
 Cols. I- I are missing.

to their purposes,

 Thou hast created the earth by Thy power 
and the seas and deeps [by Thy might].

according to Thy wisdom, 
and hast appointed al that is in them 
according to Thy wi l.
 [And] to the spirit of man 
which Thou hast formed in the world,
